Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd sit down and get through the readings, but most days I just couldn't bring myself to do it. When I did study, it was usually late at night when the anxiety about falling behind got too intense to ignore. I'd skim through materials for a few hours, maybe take some notes, but there was no real structure to it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when a deadline was coming up and try to cram everything in. Regular study times just never stuck for me—I'd set alarms, make plans, but then something would come up or I'd just feel too drained to follow through. Life stuff kept getting in the way, and I'd lose track of what was even due.
Q3: Usually it was fear, if I'm being honest. I'd get an email reminder about a deadline or realize I hadn't logged in for over a week, and the anxiety would push me to check what I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to see how far behind I was, which usually made me feel worse. It wasn't curiosity or interest—it was more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a week or two early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every other day. But it never stuck. The platform felt like another chore, and once I fell behind, logging in just reminded me of everything I wasn't doing. It became something I avoided rather than something routine.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful because that's how I learn best—just reading and taking notes in my own time. When there were clear, well-organized PDFs or articles, I could download them and work through them at my own pace. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful too, seeing how other people interpreted things, though I didn't participate much myself.
Q6: I basically ignored the videos. They took too long and I couldn't focus on them—my mind would wander and I'd have to rewind constantly. A lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials I skipped entirely because I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. When you're barely treading water, the extras feel impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to an assignment or exam. If I couldn't see an immediate payoff, I'd put it off indefinitely. I also needed things to be clear and straightforward—if something felt confusing or vague about what I was supposed to get out of it, I'd lose interest fast. My bandwidth was limited, so everything had to justify itself.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff? I'd think 'that would be nice' and then never get to it. There was always something more urgent, and optional things just slipped off my radar entirely.
Q9: Yeah, a lot of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download the PDFs and readings, then work through them on my laptop without being connected. I have limited resources, so I'd often study at places without reliable internet. I also used a notebook constantly—writing things out by hand helped me process the information better than just staring at a screen.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was going over downloaded materials or reviewing my handwritten notes for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. Sometimes I'd read the same PDF three or four times trying to understand a concept, all offline. Or I'd be working through practice problems I'd printed out. None of that activity would register anywhere.
Q11: Definitely. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for a topic, and then not touch the LMS again for that material. It was more efficient that way—I didn't have to deal with slow loading times or distractions. Once I had the files, I could study them on my own terms, on the bus or wherever, without needing to be online.
Q12: There were stretches where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed—I knew I needed to engage but I just couldn't make myself do it. Financial stress was constant, and sometimes I had to prioritize work over school. The boredom was real too; when the material didn't grab me, forcing myself to care felt like pushing a boulder uphill.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Fatigue made everything harder—I'd log in, stare at the screen, and realize I wasn't absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant coursework always took a backseat. The confusion didn't help either; sometimes I wasn't even sure what I was supposed to be doing.
Q14: Yeah, that happened a lot. I'd open up a lecture or reading and just... zone out. My eyes would move across the page but nothing was sinking in. I'd finish a video and realize I couldn't tell you a single thing it covered. Those sessions felt like going through the motions, checking a box without actually learning anything.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important, but the thought of starting it would trigger this wave of anxiety, so I'd avoid it. The bigger and more important something was, the more I'd procrastinate. It's like my brain associated those tasks with stress, so it would do anything to delay facing them. Not logical, but that's how it worked.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then scramble in the last 24 hours before something was due. It wasn't healthy—I'd pull late nights, rush through things, submit work I wasn't proud of. But without that pressure, I couldn't motivate myself. The deadline was my external structure since I couldn't create my own.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I got encouraging comments, it would give me a brief boost, make me think maybe I could do this. But if feedback was critical or I got a bad grade, it would send me into a spiral and I'd avoid the course even more. So feedback affected me, but not always in productive ways. Sometimes ignorance felt safer.
Q18: The course structure was pretty loose, which sounds nice but was actually terrible for someone like me. I needed more scaffolding, more check-ins, more forced accountability. When things were spread out with distant deadlines, I'd drift. The flexibility that probably helped some students just gave me more rope to hang myself with, metaphorically speaking.
Q19: Having everything in one place was helpful when I could bring myself to log in. But the lack of clear weekly milestones made it easy to fall behind without realizing how bad it had gotten. More frequent, smaller deadlines would have kept me on track. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and awkward, which made me participate minimally.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's trying but struggling to produce. Maybe they're reading everything, watching videos, genuinely attempting to learn, but when it comes to assignments they freeze up or run out of time. Or they could be anxious like me—constantly checking what's due, what they've missed, without being able to actually complete things. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's basically me. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline a lot and did okay on exams because I can absorb information from reading. My exam scores were decent even though my attendance and LMS activity were terrible. The system probably flagged me as disengaged, but I was learning—just not in ways that showed up in the data.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline work, the thinking you do away from the screen, the times you're genuinely engaged versus just going through motions. They're a measure of platform interaction, not learning. Someone could game the system by clicking around without learning anything.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, thinking about it even when you're not in class, wanting to understand rather than just pass. It's mental investment. By that definition, I wasn't very engaged this semester—I was surviving, not thriving. I did what I had to do to get through, but the genuine intellectual curiosity wasn't there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety, the hours I spent worrying about the course without actually working on it. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the financial stress that pulled my attention away, or the mindfulness exercises I did to try to cope. It wouldn't show that I actually understood the material better than my participation suggested. The data tells a story, but it's not the whole story.
